edges.description: IL-1-driven autoinflammation produces the systemic inflammatory response (fever, arthralgia, bone pain, acute-phase reactant elevation).

nodes.description: Schnitzler syndrome is now regarded as an acquired adult-onset autoinflammatory disease in which dysregulated innate immunity drives systemic inflammation. Interleukin-1 (especially IL-1 beta) is the pivotal mediator; the near-pathognomonic response to IL-1 inhibitors demonstrates the central role of this cytokine in disease pathogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Schnitzler Syndrome: Insights into Its Pathogenesis, Clinical Manifestations, and Current Management.
evidence.snippet: Schnitzler syndrome shares strong clinicopathologic similarities with monogenic IL-1-mediated autoinflammatory disorders and is now considered an acquired adult-onset autoinflammatory disease.

evidence.reference_title: Schnitzler Syndrome: Insights into Its Pathogenesis, Clinical Manifestations, and Current Management.
evidence.snippet: The spectacular effect of interleukin-1 inhibitors demonstrates the key role of this cytokine in the pathogenesis of the disease.
the pivotal role of interleukin-1 beta in this disorder

nodes.description: Sustained IL-1-driven inflammation produces a chronic systemic inflammatory state with fever, arthralgia, bone pain, and markedly elevated acute-phase reactants (CRP, serum amyloid A). This is the clinical readout of the autoinflammatory cascade and the target of disease-activity monitoring.

evidence.snippet: signs and symptoms of systemic inflammation, including fever, arthralgias and bone pain
